content.problemStatement:
A **union** in C stores different data types in the **same memory location**.

This means assigning a new member overwrites the previously stored value.

You must simulate a union `Test` containing:


int a
float b
char c
You will receive:


An integer
A float
A character
After assigning each value (in the above order), print the values of all members.

Since a union shares memory, only the **last assigned value** is valid; others become **garbage**.


Input Format
Input contains three lines of inputs:



First line contains an Integer `a`
Second line contains a Float `b`
Third line contains a Character `c`


Output Format
Print three lines:


`After assigning int: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
After assigning float: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
After assigning char: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
`
Use the word `garbage` for invalid values and for float values print it up to two decimal places.
constraints: c is a printable ASCII character
content.inputFormat:
Three lines of input:
First line contains an integer a.
Second line contains a floating‑point number b.
Third line contains a character c.
content.outputFormat:
Print three lines:
After assigning int: a = <value>, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = <value>, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = <value>
Print the floating‑point value with exactly two digits after the decimal point.

sampleTestCases.output: After assigning int: a = 10, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 3.50, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = Z
sampleTestCases.explanation: The integer 10 is read first and printed with garbage for the other members. Then 3.5 is read and printed as the valid float. Finally character Z is read and printed as the valid char.

sampleTestCases.output: After assigning int: a = 1000, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = A
sampleTestCases.explanation: Demonstrates handling of the maximum allowed integer and a zero float.

sampleTestCases.output: After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 1.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = 1
sampleTestCases.explanation: All three values are distinct but look similar; the program treats them according to their type.

hidden.label: minimum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = a

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: After assigning int: a = 1000, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 1000.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = ~

hidden.label: uniform/edge case
hidden.expectedOutput: After assigning int: a = 5, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 5.55, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = 5

hidden.label: Case 4
hidden.expectedOutput: After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = a

hidden.label: Case 5
hidden.expectedOutput: After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage\nAfter assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage\nAfter assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = a

solution.approach: Read the three values in the given order. After reading each value, output a line that shows the just‑assigned member with its actual value and the other two members as "garbage". The float is printed with two decimal places. The algorithm runs in O(1) time and O(1) extra space.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if len(data) < 3:
        return
    a = int(data[0].strip())
    print(f"After assigning int: a = {a}, b = garbage, c = garbage")
    b = float(data[1].strip())
    print(f"After assigning float: a = garbage, b = {b:.2f}, c = garbage")
    c = data[2].strip()[0]
    print(f"After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = {c}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
